coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescence resonance energy transfer for the prostate specific antigen (PSA) with high sensitivity
coredata.dc:creator: He, Jia Hui
Cheng, Yun Ying
Zhang, Qian Qian
Liu, Hui
Huang, Cheng Zhi
coredata.dc:description: The accurate and sensitive detection of biomarkers has great clinical value for the early diagnosis and treatment of cancer. Due to the excellent optical properties of carbon dots (CDs), CDs-based fluorescent detection methods have attracted increasing attention in bioanalytics. Signal reporters using CDs labeled hairpin DNA, based on Föster resonance energy transfer (FRET), have shown promise for the sensitive detection of biomarkers. In this work, a new method for sensitive biomarker detection using an enzyme-free amplified fluorescence strategy was developed. The strategy was based on FRET between CDs and graphene oxide (GO) combined with catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA). In the absence of the target, the CD-labeled hairpin DNA adsorb onto GO via hydrophobic and π-π stacking interactions, resulting in a FRET quenching of the CDs fluorescence. The introduction of the target could trigger the CHA circuits to form Y-shaped double-stranded DNA (dsDNA), resulting in a recovery of the CD's fluorescence signal. This novel strategy was successfully applied for the selective detection of prostate specific antigen (PSA) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.22 ng mL−1 (3σ/k). Additionally, the method could be used to detect carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and adenosine triphosphate (ATP) with LOD of 0.56 ng mL−1 (3σ/k) and 80 nM (3σ/k), respectively. Therefore, this work demonstrates a promising way to construct a sensitive and versatile detection platform.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Biomarkers
PSA
FRET
CHA

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the DNA hybridization and detection for PSA
Biomarker fluorescence detection principle and feasibility study
Sensitivity and specificity detection of PSA
The feasibility of the developed strategy in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( NSFC , Grant No. 21976144 , No. 21705132 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the CDs and feasibility study. (a) TEM image of CDs (inset: particle size distribution and HRTEM image); (b) The absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs: UV–Vis spectra (black line), excitation spectra (red line) and emission spectra (blue line); (c) Agarose gel electrophoresis analysis: lane 1, DNA marker; lane 2, T; lane 3, B; lane 4, T + B; lane 5, H1; lane 6, CDs-DNA (H2); lane 7, H3; lane 8, T + B + H1+CDs-DNA (H2)+H3; lane 9, T + B + PSA + H1+CDs-DNA (H2)+H3. (d) The fluorescence lifetime decay of CDs in the absence or presence of PSA. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Optimization of the experimental conditions. The effect of (a) the concentration of GO, (b) the length of the blocker complementary with aptamer, (c) incubating temperature and (d) incubating time. F/F 0, where F is the fluorescence emission intensity in the presence of the target PSA, and F 0 is the fluorescence emission intensity in the absence of target PSA. Experimental conditions: T, 200 nM; B, 500 nM; H1, 200 nM; H3, 200 nM; CDs-DNA (H2), 20 μL; PSA, 50 ng mL−1; GO, 70 μg mL−1 λex, 360 nm; λem, 466 nm. Error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.

The sensitivity of FRET-based PSA detection. (a) The fluorescence emission intensity in the presence of different concentrates of PSA in the range of 0.1–100 ng mL−1 (0.1, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 5, 7.5, 10, 25, 50 and 100 ng mL−1); (b) the linear relationship between F/F 0 and PSA concentration, F/F 0 = 0.043 c PSA + 2.59, r2 = 0.993, the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.20 ng mL−1 (3σ/k). Experimental conditions: GO, 70 μg mL−1; 90 min of incubation time at 37 °C; 25 min of quenching time. λex, 360 nm; λem, 466 nm. Error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.

The sensitivity for CEA and ATP detection. (a) The fluorescence emission intensity in the presence of different concentrates of CEA in the range of 0.1–100 ng mL−1 (0.1, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 5, 10, 25, 50 and 100 ng mL−1); (b) the linear relationship between F/F 0 and the CEA concentration, F/F 0 = 0.032 c CEA + 2.17, r2 = 0.995, an LOD of 0.56 ng mL−1 (3σ/k); (c) the fluorescence emission intensity in presence of different concentrations of ATP in the range of 0.1–15 μM (0.1, 1, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 15 μM) and (d) the linear relationship between F/F 0 and the ATP concentration, F/F 0 = 0.557 c ATP + 1.3, r2 = 0.997, an LOD of 80 nM (3σ/k). Experimental conditions: GO, 70 μg mL−1; 90 min of incubation time at 37 °C; 25 min of quenching time. λex, 360 nm; λem, 466 nm. Error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.
Schematic illustration of the design strategy for the sensitive detection of biomarkers using signal amplification based on FRET and the CHA circuit.
Determination of PSA in male volunteers’ serum.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescence resonance energy transfer for the prostate specific antigen (PSA) with high sensitivity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical System (Southwest University), Chongqing Science and Technology Bureau, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical System (Southwest University), Chongqing Science and Technology Bureau, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical System (Southwest University), Chongqing Science and Technology Bureau, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical System (Southwest University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The accurate and sensitive detection of biomarkers has great clinical value for the early diagnosis and treatment of cancer. Due to the excellent optical properties of carbon dots (CDs), CDs-based fluorescent detection methods have attracted increasing attention in bioanalytics. Signal reporters using CDs labeled hairpin DNA, based on Föster resonance energy transfer (FRET), have shown promise for the sensitive detection of biomarkers. In this work, a new method for sensitive biomarker detection using an enzyme-free amplified fluorescence strategy was developed. The strategy was based on FRET between CDs and graphene oxide (GO) combined with catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA). In the absence of the target, the CD-labeled hairpin DNA adsorb onto GO via hydrophobic and π-π stacking interactions, resulting in a FRET quenching of the CDs fluorescence. The introduction of the target could trigger the CHA circuits to form Y-shaped double-stranded DNA (dsDNA), resulting in a recovery of the CD's fluorescence signal. This novel strategy was successfully applied for the selective detection of prostate specific antigen (PSA) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.22 ng mL−1 (3σ/k). Additionally, the method could be used to detect carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and adenosine triphosphate (ATP) with LOD of 0.56 ng mL−1 (3σ/k) and 80 nM (3σ/k), respectively. Therefore, this work demonstrates a promising way to construct a sensitive and versatile detection platform.

A detection system for sensitive biomarker determination was accomplished through the combination of the FRET-based CDs/GO and CHA signal amplification strategies.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a leading cause of mortality worldwide. The uncontrolled growth and spread of abnormal cells often invade surrounding tissue and can metastasize to distant parts of the body [1]. Low survival rates are generally caused by late-stage diagnosis and limited access to timely and effective treatment. Hence, early detection can result in lower mortality rates through timely treatment. In this light, methods for the early and accurate detection/diagnosis of cancer is crucial for its successful management [2,3]. A biomarker is defined as “a characteristic that is objectively measured and evaluated as an indicator of normal biological processes, pathogenic processes, or pharmacological responses to a therapeutic intervention” [4]. Biomarkers, as a tool, form the foundation of cancer detection and monitoring [4–7]. For example, PSA is considered a biomarker for diagnosing prostate cancer, and CEA is an important biomarker to colorectal and gastrointestinal carcinomas.

At present, several methods have been developed to detect biomarkers, which include the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [5,6], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [7–9], electrochemical immunoassays [10,11], and fluorescence-based assays [12–14]. Among these methods, fluorescence-based assays have attracted significant attention owing to their simple quantification process, the easy detection of large-scale samples, and rapidity. To achieve high sensitivity, the Föster resonance energy transfer (FRET) detection strategy is widely used for the detection of biomarkers [15]. FRET is a non-radiative phenomenon where the energy from a fluorescent donor in the excited state is transferred to a fluorescent acceptor by means of intermolecular dipole-dipole coupling. The donor molecules typically emit at shorter wavelengths that spectrally overlap with the absorption of the acceptor. The distance between the energy pairs is typically between 10 Å–100 Å [16–18]. FRET has been widely used in fluorescence-based applications, such as in medical diagnostics, biological analysis, and bioimaging [19–24].

The rational selection of an energy donor and acceptor is a key factor in the construction of a FRET system. Organic fluorescent dye molecules are frequently used in FRET systems. This is due to their advantages of good solubility, high quantum yield, and easy chemical modification; however, their shortcomings, which include poor photobleaching resistance, high pH sensitivity, and biological toxicity limit their application. Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a promising alternative to organic dyes. This is because of their unique optical properties, excellent photostability, good water solubility, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility. These advantages lead to them being good candidates as donors in FRET systems for the detection of various biomolecules, and in biomedical and bioimaging applications [25–28]. Graphene oxide (GO) is a two dimensional (2D) graphitic carbon system that consists of sp2 carbon clusters and an sp3 C–O matrix. It has recently attracted significant attention due to its wide absorption band and distinct electronic, optical, and mechanical properties. GO's unique heterogeneous electronic structure and wide absorption band makes it an excellent nano-quenching agent for common fluorescent substances [29,30]. In addition, single-strand DNA (ssDNA) and hairpin DNA can adsorb onto GO via hydrophobic and π–π stacking interactions [31–34]. Therefore, GO is an efficient energy acceptor for use in FRET assays.

Several different signal amplification strategies have been introduced in recent years to increase the sensitivity of FRET assays. In particular, catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) has attracted great interest due to its hybridization process that can be programmed to self-assemble without the need for enzymes; furthermore, it is a simple and efficient signal amplification strategy [35–38].

In this work, we developed a detection system for sensitive biomarker determination. This was accomplished through the combination of the FRET-based CDs/GO and CHA signal amplification strategies. As shown in Scheme 1 , the CDs-hairpins can be closely adsorbed onto the GO surface via hydrophobic and π–π stacking interactions; following adsorption, the fluorescence of the CDs was quenched by GO through FRET. On introduction of the target molecule, the rigid, Y-shaped double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) structure that forms desorbs from the GO surface. This results in a recovery of the fluorescence signal.

All the DNA oligonucleotides used in this work were synthesized and purified by Sangon (Shanghai, China). The hairpins and DNA sequences were heated to 95 °C for 5 min and then allowed to cool to room temperature for at least 2 h before use. The detailed sequences information for all the DNAs are listed in Table S1. Detailed DNA and RNA sequences are shown in Table S1.PSA and CEA were purchased from Shangha Linc-Bio Science Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). ATP was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Agarose powder was purchased from Hongkong Gene Co. Ltd. The DNA stock solution was prepared with ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ) from a Millipore Milli-Q water purification system (Billerica, MA). GO was purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Inc. (Nanjing, China) and suspended in water via sonication. 20X PBS was purchased from Sangon (Shanghai, China). The hybridization buffer, 30 mM Tris-HCl, 300 mM KCl, and 4 mM MgCl2, pH 7.8. 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, purity 98%), and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, purity 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and Di Bai Chemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China), respectively.

The fluorescence (FL) and UV–vis absorption spectra are recorded with an F-2500 FL spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) and a UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan), respectively. Zeta potentials are measured by dynamic laser light scattering (ZEN3600, Malvern). The fluorescence lifetime is measured with an FL-TCSPC spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France). Solutions were blended using a vortex mixer QL- 901 (Haimen, China) before each measurement. An Eppendorf ThermoMixer C (Jiangsu, China) was used for controlling temperature. Electrophoresis was performed on DYCP-31DN agarose electrophoresis tank (Liuyi, Beijing, China).

The CDs were synthesized according to our previously reported work [39]. For the synthetic method for the CDs-DNA refer to the report by Wang et al. work [40]. In brief, the as-prepared CDs were first dissolved in 5 mL of PBS buffer (1X) to obtain a CDs suspension (0.1 mg mL−1). The solution pH was then adjusted to pH 5 for protonation of the carboxyl groups of the CDs. After that, a mixture of EDC (19 mg) and NHS (22 mg) was added into the solution to activate the CD carboxylic groups stirring of 30 min at room temperature. Then, the pH of the CDs suspension was adjusted to pH 7. The CDs-DNA conjugates were obtained by adding 24 μL of amino-modified DNA (100 μM) into the above activated solution under continuous stirring for 2 h at room temperature to carry out the condensation reaction. The obtained CDs-DNA conjugate solution was maintained at 4 °C overnight and then centrifuged at a speed of 12000 rpm for 20 min using centrifugal ultrafiltration tubes and stored at 4 °C in the dark until use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the DNA hybridization and detection for PSA
ce:section.ce:para: First, 0.2 μM DNA T and 0.5 μM DNA B were mixed and incubated for 1h at 37 °C in the hybridization buffer to form the T-B duplex. Then, different concentrations of PSA and 0.2 μM H1, 0.2 μM H3, and 20 μL CDs-DNA were mixed with the T-B solution incubated at 37 °C for 90 min. After that, GO was added to the above solution and incubated at 25 °C for another 20 min before detection. The control experiments were carried out under the same conditions without adding PSA. The detection of CEA and ATP was also conducted in a similar manner.

We first characterized our synthesized CDs and CDs-DNA (H2). In our previous work [39], we successfully synthesized CDs with abundant carboxyl functional groups; this resulted in excellent water solubility and provided reaction groups for subsequent condensation with amino-modified DNA. In this work, the as-prepared CDs were well dispersed and had an average size of ~1.98 nm. The lattice fringes of the CDs were well-resolved with a spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1 a) that can be attributed to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon. The two typical absorption bands observed at 247 and 350 nm are attributed to the π→π* transition of CC and the n→π* transition of CO, respectively. The maximum fluorescence emission was located at 466 nm when excited at 360 nm (Fig. 1b). After modifying DNA on the surface of the as-prepared CDs, we measured the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CDs and the CDs-DNA. An obvious absorption peak of the CDs-DNA was observed at 260 nm (Fig. S1a, ESI†) and can be attributed to the characteristic absorption of DNA. The zeta potentials of the CDs, DNA, and CDs-DNA were also investigated (Fig. S1b, ESI†) and determined to be −5.6 mV, −7.1 mV and −27.8 mV, respectively. Moreover, the electrophoretic mobility determined by agarose gel electrophoresis was smaller for CDs-DNA (H2) than for DNA (H2) (Fig. S1c, ESI†). The above data clearly confirm that the CDs surfaces was conjugated with DNA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biomarker fluorescence detection principle and feasibility study
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrates the principle for the amplified detection of biomarkers based on the CDs/GO FRET and the CHA circuit. The target aptamer-based trigger DNA (T) was inhibited by a blocker DNA (B). In the absence of the target, the trigger DNA failed to initiate the CHA circuit. The sticky single strand end of the hairpins has a strong non-covalent binding interaction with GO via hydrophobic and π-π stacking interactions. This leads to a strong quenching of the CDs fluorescence signal. When the target binds to the aptamer-based trigger, the T-B duplex is destabilized and the T triggers a dynamic assembly between the hairpin H1, CDs-DNA (H2), and H3. A continuous Y-shaped dsDNA structure is formed, which has a weak binding interaction with GO. This results in a recovery of the fluorescence of the CDs, hence, the target can be sensitively and specifically detected by the significantly higher fluorescence intensity. To verify the feasibility of the proposed method, we chose PSA as a model target. As shown in Fig. 1c, in the absence of PSA there was minimal assembly of the probes (lane 8). However, an obvious Y-shaped dsDNA structure was observed in the presence of PSA (lane 9). Moreover, on measuring the CDs lifetime, an obvious change was observed from 7.02 ns to 2.42 ns for the samples with and without added GO, respectively. At the same time, the lifetime of the CDs increased to 5.10 ns in the presence of PSA (Fig. 1d). Thus, it was determined that a detection platform for PSA based on FRET and the CHA circuit is feasible.

In order to achieve better analytical performance, we first examined the effects of the GO concentrations on the fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the fluorescence ratio (F/F 0) gradually increased as the GO concentration went from 10 to 70 μg mL−1. This could be due to the adsorption/desorption of the hairpin/Y-shaped dsDNA structure on GO. However, excessive amounts of GO quenched the CDs fluorescence, leading to a decrease in the F/F 0. Therefore, 70 μg mL−1 was chosen as the optimal GO concentration. The signal amplification process was strongly affected by the number of bases of B that are complementary with T for DNA self-assembly. Four kinds of B sequences (B-11, B-13, B-15, and B-17 bases in Table S1) with different lengths were designed. When B was too short, its hybridization with T was unstable; contrarily, if B was too long, it would not release from T on PSA addition. When the number of B bases was 15, the F/F 0 reached its maximum value (Fig. 2b). Thus, B with 15 bases was selected for use in the following experiments to maintain a balance between the hybridization and release capability. Furthermore, the effects of the incubation temperature, the time for self-assembly of the DNA hairpins, and the incubation time with GO (quenching time) were investigated. Incubation temperature is an important parameter for the reaction kinetics and influences the stability and interaction of the hairpin probes. At a lower incubation temperature, there is reduced formation of the Y-shaped dsDNA structure complexes due to the weaker molecular collisions. On increasing the incubation temperature, the hairpin probes could achieve sufficient collision velocity and, hence, more amplification products could be formed. As shown in Fig. 2c, an optimal F/F 0 is observed for the detection system at 37 °C and was used the incubation temperature for the subsequent experiments. The incubation time is another important parameter to optimize the performance of this probe. The incubation time was investigated in range of 30–120 min. The fluorescence signals were detected at different time points during incubation. The result of the incubation time optimization are shown in Fig. 2d. F/F 0 increased with increasing incubation time and stabilized at times above 60 min. Therefore, 90 min was chosen as an optimal incubation time. In addition, the quenching time was also optimized. It was observed that F/F 0 reached a maximum at 25 min and then decreased gradually at longer quenching times (Fig. S2, ESI†). Therefore, 25 min was selected as the optimal quenching time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and specificity detection of PSA
ce:section.ce:para: Using the determined optimal experimental conditions, different concentrations of PSA were measured to evaluate the analytical performance of the designed fluorescence detection platform. As shown in Fig. 3 a, on increasing the PSA concentration, the fluorescence signal gradually recovered. There was a good linear relationship between F/F 0 and the PSA concentration in the range of 1–100 ng mL−1 (Fig. 3b). A correlation coefficient of 0.993 and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.22 ng mL−1 (3σ/k) were determined. The specificity is another important metric for evaluating analytical performance and was tested for the detection system. It could be determined that only PSA induces an obvious increase in the F/F 0; while there was little observable change in F/F 0 in the presence of other proteins (HSA, Hb, IgG, and CEA), even though their concentrations were ten times higher than that of PSA (Fig. S3, ESI†). These results indicate that the designed fluorescence detection platform has high selectivity and specificity towards PSA in aqueous solution over other proteins.

In order to demonstrate the universality of the method, it was next applied to detect carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and adenosine triphosphate (ATP). DNA T2/T3 was designed, which contains the CEA/ATP aptamer sequence and hybridizes with B2/B3. In the presence of ATP/CEA, the strong, specific binding between the aptamer and DNA T2/T3 trigger CHA to emit a fluorescence signal. The linear range for CEA/ATP was between 1 and 100 ng mL−1 or 0.1–10 μM (Fig. 4 a–d). The linear regression equations were F/F 0 = 0.0317 c CEA + 2.17 and F/F 0 = 0.557 c ATP + 1.3, with correlation coefficients of r2 = 0.991 and r2 = 0.995, respectively. The LODs were 0.56 ng mL−1 (3σ/k) and 80 nM (3σ/k) for CEA and ATP, respectively. The specificity was also investigated under the same experimental conditions. It was found that none of these substances caused obvious alteration of the change in F/F 0 induced by CEA or ATP (Figs. S4a–b, ESI†). Thus, this method could be used to detect various biomarkers, constructing a sensitive and versatile detection platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility of the developed strategy in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Next, the practicality and accuracy of the developed array for the analysis of real-world samples was investigated. Serum samples from male volunteers were validated by analysis of PSA. The standard addition method was applied to the serum samples. To certify the accuracy and precision of the method a recovery experiment was conducted. When 1.0 ng mL−1, 5.0 ng mL−1, and 10.0 ng mL−1 of PSA were added into the sample solution, respectively, the average recoveries and the RSD values in the three samples were in the 90–110% and 1.2–6.7% range, respectively (Table 1 ). The results demonstrate that the high reproducibility of the method in real-world samples.

Compared with other reported methods (Table S2, ESI†), this method is suitable for selective and sensitive analysis of PSA of prostate cancer patients, whose PSA concentration are of 4–10 ng mL−1. Moreover, our method has obvious advantages such as simple, highly selective and universal.

In summary, we have developed a simple fluorescence-based method for the sensitive detection of PSA on the basis of CDs/GO FRET and the CHA circuit. Three hairpin DNA were designed to self-assemble on the detection platform (Y-shaped dsDN product). This detection strategy achieved a better sensitivity and specificity. Additionally, this assay was adapted to detect other targets such as CEA and ATP. This demonstrated its applicability as a universal detection method that holds great potential for clinical diagnosis and biomedical research studies.

Jia Hui He: Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yun Ying Cheng: Methodology, Software, Resources. Qian Qian Zhang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Hui Liu: Conceptualization, Supervision. Cheng Zhi Huang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant No. 21976144, No. 21705132).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121276.
We declare that no conflict of interest exits in the submission of this manuscript, and the manuscript has been approved by all authors for publication.

Choo, Simultaneous detection of dual prostate specific antigens using surface-enhanced raman scattering-based immunoassay for accurate diagnosis of prostate cancer, ACS Nano 11 (2017) 4926-4933.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous detection of dual prostate specific antigens using surface-enhanced Raman scattering-based immunoassay for accurate diagnosis of prostate cancer

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive and selective detection of a prognostic indicator in early-stage cancer using graphene oxide and carbon nanotubes

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface-engineered quantum dots/electrospun nanofibers as a networked fluorescence aptasensing platform toward biomarker
